title: Myxedema Coma
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Endocrinology Fellows, Critical Care, Emergency Medicine
objectives: Recognize clinical features of myxedema coma
Initiate appropriate treatment (hydrocortisone BEFORE thyroid hormone)
Select thyroid hormone replacement regimen (T4 ± T3)
Provide supportive care (avoid active rewarming, ventilatory support)

guidelineReferences.name: ATA Guidelines for Treatment of Hypothyroidism
guidelineReferences.citation: Jonklaas J, et al. Guidelines for the Treatment of Hypothyroidism. Thyroid. 2014;24(12):1670-1751

vignette.text:
A 72-year-old woman is brought to the ED by EMS after being found unresponsive at home during a cold snap. Her family reports she has been increasingly confused and lethargic over the past week. She has a history of hypothyroidism but ran out of her levothyroxine 3 months ago and didn't refill it.

Past Medical History: Hypothyroidism (post-radioiodine ablation for Graves disease 15 years ago), Type 2 diabetes, Hypertension

Medications: Was on levothyroxine 125 mcg daily (not taking), metformin, lisinopril

Allergies: NKDA

physicalExam.general: Obtunded, responds only to painful stimuli, marked periorbital and facial edema
physicalExam.HEENT: Macroglossia, periorbital edema, thin lateral eyebrows
physicalExam.extremities: Non-pitting edema, cool and dry skin, delayed relaxation of reflexes

diagnostics.ECG: Sinus bradycardia 48 bpm, low voltage QRS, prolonged QT

questions.stem: This patient has hypothermia, bradycardia, altered mental status, and undetectable thyroid hormones.
questions.question: What is the classic clinical triad of myxedema coma?

options.text: Classic triad: (1) ALTERED MENTAL STATUS (confusion → obtundation → coma), (2) HYPOTHERMIA (often <95°F/35°C), (3) PRECIPITATING EVENT (infection, cold exposure, medication non-adherence, sedatives, surgery); Additional features: Bradycardia, hypotension, hypoventilation, hyponatremia, hypoglycemia
Fever, tachycardia, and agitation
Patients are always in true coma
Only laboratory findings matter

questions.explanation: Myxedema coma triad: (1) ALTERED MENTAL STATUS: Despite name 'coma,' most patients have delirium/obtundation rather than true coma - progressive AMS is hallmark; (2) HYPOTHERMIA: Present in 70-90%, may be severe (<94°F), may be masked by concurrent infection; (3) PRECIPITATING EVENT: Something overwhelms compensatory mechanisms. Additional classic features: Bradycardia, hypotension, hypoventilation with respiratory failure, hyponatremia (SIADH-like), hypoglycemia, non-pitting edema, delayed DTRs. This patient has all features: obtunded (AMS), temp 94.5°F (hypothermia), medication non-adherence (precipitant).

whyChain.answer: Hypothyroidism causes reduced cardiac output (bradycardia + reduced contractility), but chronically patients compensate with peripheral vasoconstriction. When decompensated, this fails. Also: Pericardial effusion (distant heart sounds, cardiomegaly on CXR) further reduces cardiac output. Hypotension may be refractory to fluids and vasopressors until thyroid hormone is given.

questions.stem: This patient stopped taking her levothyroxine 3 months ago.
questions.question: What are common precipitating factors for myxedema coma?

options.text: DIMES mnemonic: Drugs (sedatives, opioids, anesthetics, amiodarone, lithium), Infection (pneumonia, UTI, sepsis - most common), Metabolic (hypoglycemia, hyponatremia, acidosis), Environment (cold exposure), Stroke/Surgery/Stress (MI, trauma, burns); Also: Thyroid hormone non-adherence, GI bleeding, diuretics
Only cold exposure causes myxedema coma
Precipitating factors are not important
Thyroid hormone non-adherence is rare

questions.explanation: Precipitating factors (DIMES): DRUGS: Sedatives, opioids, anesthetics, amiodarone, lithium, beta-blockers, antipsychotics, checkpoint inhibitors; INFECTION: Most common precipitant - pneumonia, UTI, sepsis (may lack fever due to hypothermia); METABOLIC: Hypoglycemia, hyponatremia, acidosis; ENVIRONMENT: Cold exposure (especially in elderly during winter); STROKE/SURGERY/STRESS: CVA, MI, trauma, surgery, burns, GI bleeding. ALSO: Thyroid hormone non-adherence (this patient), contrast dyes. This patient: Non-adherence + cold exposure = double precipitant.

questions.stem: Labs show TSH 95, undetectable T4 and T3.
questions.question: How is myxedema coma diagnosed?

options.text: Clinical diagnosis supported by labs: HIGH TSH + LOW free T4 (primary hypothyroidism - most common); Low/normal TSH + low T4 = central hypothyroidism (pituitary); Labs also show: Hyponatremia (dilutional), hypoglycemia, elevated CPK, hypercholesterolemia, anemia; ABG: Respiratory acidosis with hypoxemia; ECG: Bradycardia, low voltage, prolonged QT; Start treatment empirically if high suspicion - don't wait for labs
TSH is always normal in myxedema coma
Diagnosis requires confirmed coma
Wait for all labs before treating

questions.explanation: Myxedema coma diagnosis: CLINICAL SUSPICION is key - don't wait for labs to treat. Lab findings: PRIMARY HYPOTHYROIDISM (95%): High TSH (often >50), Low/undetectable free T4/T3; CENTRAL (pituitary): Low/inappropriate normal TSH, Low T4/T3. Associated labs: Hyponatremia (impaired free water clearance), Hypoglycemia (reduced gluconeogenesis ± adrenal insufficiency), Elevated CPK (myopathy), Hypercholesterolemia, Normocytic anemia. ABG: Respiratory acidosis (hypoventilation) + hypoxemia. ECG: Bradycardia, low voltage (pericardial effusion), prolonged QT. This patient: Primary hypothyroidism (TSH 95, undetectable T4).

whyChain.answer: Hypothyroidism can coexist with adrenal insufficiency: (1) Central: Pituitary disease affects both TSH and ACTH; (2) Autoimmune: Schmidt syndrome (autoimmune thyroiditis + adrenal insufficiency). Also, giving thyroid hormone increases cortisol metabolism - can precipitate adrenal crisis if underlying insufficiency. ALWAYS give hydrocortisone before/with thyroid hormone.

questions.stem: You are preparing to treat this patient.
questions.question: Why must glucocorticoids be given BEFORE thyroid hormone?

options.text: HYDROCORTISONE 100 mg IV q8h (200-300 mg/day total) - give BEFORE thyroid hormone; Rationale: (1) Thyroid hormone increases cortisol metabolism → can precipitate adrenal crisis; (2) Hypothyroidism can mask coexisting adrenal insufficiency; (3) Draw random cortisol first but don't wait for results; Taper once cortisol confirmed adequate and patient stabilized
Glucocorticoids are not needed
Give thyroid hormone first, steroids later
Only give steroids if cortisol is low

questions.explanation: Glucocorticoid therapy in myxedema coma: HYDROCORTISONE 100 mg IV loading, then 100 mg IV q8h (or 50 mg q6h). MUST give BEFORE or WITH thyroid hormone replacement. Rationale: (1) Thyroid hormone increases cortisol metabolism - can unmask/precipitate adrenal insufficiency; (2) Hypothyroidism may mask underlying adrenal insufficiency (autoimmune polyglandular syndrome, pituitary disease); (3) Adrenal crisis has high mortality. Protocol: Draw random cortisol → Give hydrocortisone → Give thyroid hormone. Interpret cortisol later: <3 μg/dL = adrenal insufficiency, >18 μg/dL = adequate; 3-18 = needs ACTH stim test after recovery. Taper steroids once patient stable and cortisol confirmed adequate.

questions.stem: After giving hydrocortisone, you need to give thyroid hormone.
questions.question: What thyroid hormone regimen is recommended for myxedema coma?

options.text: IV LEVOTHYROXINE (T4): Loading dose 200-400 mcg IV, then 50-100 mcg IV daily; Consider adding IV LIOTHYRONINE (T3): 5-20 mcg loading, then 2.5-10 mcg q8h - for faster onset, especially if not improving; IV route preferred (GI absorption unreliable); Monitor for cardiac ischemia/arrhythmias - use lower doses in CAD/elderly; Switch to oral when patient able to take PO
Oral levothyroxine only
T3 alone is always preferred
Start with very low doses only

questions.explanation: Thyroid hormone replacement: IV route essential (unreliable GI absorption). LEVOTHYROXINE (T4): Loading 200-400 mcg IV (or 4 mcg/kg), then 50-100 mcg IV daily. T4 has slower onset (hours-days) but safer. Consider adding LIOTHYRONINE (T3): Loading 5-20 mcg IV, then 2.5-10 mcg q8h. T3 has faster onset (minutes-hours) but higher risk of arrhythmias/ischemia. ATA Guidelines: Strong recommendation for IV T4 + glucocorticoids; Weak recommendation to consider adding T3. Controversy exists: Some give T4 alone, some add T3. Monitor cardiac: Risk of arrhythmias, MI especially in elderly/CAD. If no improvement at 24-48h on T4 alone, add T3.

whyChain.answer: In severe illness, peripheral conversion of T4 to T3 is impaired ('sick euthyroid syndrome'). T3 is the active hormone at tissue level. If patient not responding to T4 alone (still comatose, hemodynamically unstable at 24h), adding T3 may help. But T3 carries higher cardiac risk - use cautiously.

questions.stem: The patient is hypothermic (94.5°F) and hypotensive.
questions.question: What supportive care is essential in myxedema coma?

options.text: HYPOTHERMIA: PASSIVE rewarming only (blankets) - active external rewarming causes vasodilation → shock; VENTILATION: Intubate if respiratory failure (this patient needs it - RR 8, hypoxemic, hypercarbic); HYPOTENSION: Fluids cautiously (risk of CHF), vasopressors may be refractory until thyroid hormone given; HYPONATREMIA: Fluid restriction, avoid rapid correction; HYPOGLYCEMIA: IV dextrose; INFECTION: Empiric antibiotics if suspected
Aggressive active rewarming is essential
Avoid intubation at all costs
Large volume fluid resuscitation

questions.explanation: Supportive care: (1) HYPOTHERMIA: PASSIVE rewarming ONLY (blankets, warm environment). Active external rewarming (warming blankets, heated O2) causes peripheral vasodilation → worsening hypotension/shock. Core warming (warm IV fluids, peritoneal lavage) may be considered for severe hypothermia; (2) VENTILATION: Low threshold for intubation - hypoventilation is common (this patient: RR 8, hypoxemic, hypercarbic). May need prolonged ventilation; (3) HYPOTENSION: Cautious IV fluids (risk of CHF from myxedema heart), Vasopressors often refractory until thyroid hormone takes effect; (4) HYPONATREMIA: Fluid restriction, will improve with thyroid hormone, avoid rapid correction; (5) HYPOGLYCEMIA: IV dextrose; (6) INFECTION: Search for and treat precipitating infection (empiric antibiotics if suspected).

questions.stem: You are discussing prognosis with the family.
questions.question: What is the prognosis of myxedema coma?

options.text: MORTALITY: 20-60% despite treatment (recent studies ~30-40%); Poor prognostic factors: Advanced age, persistent hypothermia, bradycardia <44 bpm, sepsis, hypotension, need for mechanical ventilation, lower GCS, higher APACHE scores; Shock and multiorgan failure are leading causes of death; Early recognition and treatment improve outcomes
Mortality is <5% with treatment
All patients recover completely
Prognosis is unrelated to treatment timing

questions.explanation: Myxedema coma prognosis: MORTALITY 20-60% historically, recent data suggest 30-40% with modern ICU care. Still a life-threatening emergency. POOR PROGNOSTIC FACTORS: Advanced age, Persistent hypothermia despite treatment, Severe bradycardia (<44 bpm), Sepsis/infection as precipitant, Persistent hypotension/shock, Need for mechanical ventilation, Lower GCS at presentation, Higher APACHE II scores, Multiorgan failure. Leading causes of death: Shock, respiratory failure, sepsis. KEY: Early recognition and prompt treatment with thyroid hormone + hydrocortisone improves survival. Delay in treatment significantly increases mortality.

questions.question: What is the complete management approach for this patient?

options.text: Immediate: ICU admission, cardiac monitoring; (1) Draw labs (TSH, T4, T3, cortisol) but don't wait for results; (2) HYDROCORTISONE 100 mg IV first; (3) LEVOTHYROXINE 200-400 mcg IV loading; (4) Intubate for respiratory failure (RR 8, hypoxia, hypercarbia); (5) Passive rewarming only; (6) IV dextrose for hypoglycemia; (7) Cautious fluids, vasopressors PRN; (8) Search for/treat precipitating infection; Continue T4 50-100 mcg IV daily, consider adding T3 if not improving
Wait for labs before any treatment
Give thyroid hormone before steroids
Active rewarming and observation only

questions.explanation: Complete management: IMMEDIATE ACTIONS: ICU admission, continuous cardiac monitoring, prepare for intubation. TREATMENT SEQUENCE: (1) Draw labs (TSH, free T4, free T3, random cortisol, CBC, CMP, ABG) - but DON'T WAIT for results; (2) HYDROCORTISONE 100 mg IV FIRST (before thyroid hormone); (3) LEVOTHYROXINE 200-400 mcg IV loading dose; (4) Consider LIOTHYRONINE 5-10 mcg IV if severe; (5) INTUBATE - this patient has respiratory failure (RR 8, pCO2 58, pO2 55); (6) PASSIVE REWARMING only - blankets, warm room, no active external warming; (7) IV DEXTROSE for glucose 52; (8) Cautious IV fluids (risk CHF), vasopressors if needed (may be refractory initially); (9) Search for precipitating infection, empiric antibiotics if suspected. ONGOING: T4 50-100 mcg IV daily, hydrocortisone 100 mg IV q8h, add T3 if no improvement at 24-48h, fluid restriction for hyponatremia, taper steroids when stable.
